sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 8

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 8:1
items.html:
Hochma is called light and Hassadim water. Bina is called upper water, and Malchut lower water.
Now we shall explain the essence of the four phases, cause and consequence, which are necessary for the activation of the complete form of the desire to receive, as above, in the secret of "the water conceived and gave birth to darkness." In Atzilut there are two discernments of light. The first discernment is called "light," which is the secret of Hochma; and the second discernment is called "water," which is the secret of Hassadim. For the first discernment extends from above downward without assistance from the side of the lower one, while the second discernment extends with assistance from the side of the lower one — therefore it is called "water," for such is the nature of light, that its foundation is above, and such is the nature of water, that its foundation is below; understand this. And in the water itself there are also two discernments: upper water, which is by the second of the four phases, and lower water, which is by the fourth of the four phases.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 8:2
items.html:
Explaining the expansion of the Light of Ein Sof into four phases, in order to reveal the vessel, which is the desire to receive.
Therefore, in every expansion of the Light of Ein Sof there are ten Sefirot. For Ein Sof, which is the secret of the root and the Emanator, is called Keter; and the light of the expansion itself is called Hochma, which is the entire measure of the expansion of the light from above, from Ein Sof. It is already known that in every expansion of light from above the desire to receive is included, as above — yet the form of the desire to receive is not revealed in actuality until the emanated awakens to desire and to draw light beyond the measure of its expansion. And so, since the desire to receive is included in potential at once in the light of the expansion, the light is bound to bring the potential into the actual. Therefore the light awakens to draw an addition of abundance, more than the measure that is in its expansion from the side of Ein Sof; and by this the desire to receive is revealed in actuality in that light, and it acquires the form of novelty — a slight disparity of form, as above — for it thereby becomes dimmer than the light, having thickened because of the novelty of form. And this part that thickened is called Bina. This is the secret of "I am Bina, mine is Gevura": in truth, Bina is a part of Hochma — that is, of the very light of the expansion of Ein Sof, as above — but because she prevailed in desire and drew abundance beyond the measure of the expansion in her from Ein Sof, she thereby acquired a disparity of form and thickened slightly from the light, and came out with a name of her own, the Sefira of Bina. And the essence of the addition of abundance that she drew from Ein Sof by the force of the prevailing of her desire is called light of Hassadim, or upper water, as above — because this light does not extend directly from Ein Sof, like the light of Hochma, but through the assistance of the emanated, which prevailed in desire, as above; therefore it rises with a name of its own, being called light of Hassadim, or water. And you now find in the Sefira of Bina that she is comprised of three discernments of light: the first is the light of Bina's own essence, which is a part of the light of Hochma, as above; the second is the thickening and the disparity of form in her, which she acquired by the prevailing of the desire, as above; and the third is the light of Hassadim, which came to her by her own drawing from Ein Sof.
Yet the vessel of reception is still not completed in this to its perfection, since Bina is of the very light of Hochma, which is exceedingly exalted, being the direct expansion of the Light of Ein Sof; hence there was revealed in Bina only a discernment of a root of the vessel of reception, and a discernment of an operator for the making of the vessel. For afterwards that light of Hassadim, which she drew by the force of her prevailing, expanded again from her, with a small addition of illumination of the light of Hochma. This expansion of the light of Hassadim is called Zeir Anpin, or HaGaT, as written in its place. And this light of the expansion likewise prevailed in its desire to draw new abundance, beyond the measure of the illumination of Hochma in its expansion from Bina. Therefore this expansion, too, is discerned as two discernments: the light of the expansion itself is called ZA, or VaK; and the discernment of the prevailing in it is called Malchut. And this is the secret of the ten Sefirot: Keter is the secret of Ein Sof; Hochma is the light of the expansion from Ein Sof; Bina is the secret of the light of Hochma that prevailed to add abundance, and thereby thickened, as above; ZA, comprising HaGaT NHY, is the secret of the light of Hassadim with illumination of Hochma expanding from Bina; and Malchut is the secret of the second prevailing, for an addition of illumination of Hochma beyond what is in ZA.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 8:3
items.html:
The four phases of the desire are the secret of the four letters of HaVaYaH, which are Keter-Hochma-Bina and Tiferet-Malchut.
This is the secret of the four letters of the four-letter name. The tip of the Yod is the secret of Ein Sof — that is, the force of the operator included in the thought of creation, "to delight His creatures," which is the secret of the vessel of Keter. The Yod is the secret of Hochma, namely the first phase, the discernment of the potential within the operator, included at once in the light of the expansion of Ein Sof. The first Hey is the secret of Bina, namely the second phase, the emergence of the potential into an operator — the light that thickened out of Hochma, as above. The Vav is the secret of Zeir Anpin, or HaGaT NHY — the expansion of the light of Hassadim that came out by Bina, as above — which is the third phase, a discernment of potential for the revealing of the operation, as above. The lower Hey of HaVaYaH is the secret of Malchut, namely the fourth phase, the revealing of the operation in the completion of the vessel of reception — which prevailed to draw an addition of abundance beyond the measure of its expansion from Bina; and by this the form of the desire to receive was fixed in its place, and the light clothes in its vessel, which is the desire to receive, completed only in this fourth phase and not before it. By this you will understand plainly that there is no light in the worlds, upper or lower, that is not ordered under the order of the four-letter name, which is the secret of the four phases above — for without this, the desire to receive, which must be in every light, is not fixed; for this desire is the place and the measure of that light, as above.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 8:4
items.html:
The letters Yod and Vav of HaVaYaH are thin, because they are discernments of potential only.
One should not ask: the Yod alludes to Hochma and the Hey to Bina, yet the whole essence of the light in the ten Sefirot is found in the Sefira of Hochma, while Bina, Zeir Anpin, and Malchut are but clothings with respect to Hochma — should not Hochma, then, have taken the largest letter in the four-letter name? The matter is this: the letters of the four-letter name do not indicate or allude to the measure and quantity of the light in the ten Sefirot; rather, they indicate the values of the activation of the vessel. For the white of the parchment of the Torah scroll alludes to the discernment of the light, and the black, which is the letters in the Torah scroll, alludes to the discernment of the quality of the vessels. Therefore Keter, being only a root of a root of a vessel, is alluded to only by the tip of the Yod. And Hochma, being the discernment of potential not yet revealed into the actual, is alluded to by the smallest of the letters, the Yod. And Bina, in whom the potential came out and was revealed into the actual, is alluded to by the broad letter, the Hey. And ZA, being only a potential for the revealing of the operation, as above, is alluded to by a long, thin letter, the Vav — the thinness indicating that the being of a vessel is still hidden in it in potential, in concealment, and the length of the line indicating that at the end of its expansion a whole and complete vessel is revealed through it. For Hochma did not suffice, in its expansion, to reveal a complete vessel, since Bina is not yet an actual vessel but the operator of the vessel, as above — which is why the leg of the Yod is short, indicating that it is still too short to reveal, by the potential hidden in it and by its expansion, a complete vessel. Malchut, too, is alluded to by the letter Hey, like the Sefira of Bina — a broad letter, revealed in completeness of form. And let it not be difficult for you that Bina and Malchut have identical letters: it is because in the world of correction they are indeed similar to one another and borrow their vessels from one another, in the secret of the verse "and they both went," as written in its place.
